Her work was driven by something deeply personal: the forests she studied were the ones she had known since she was young, and the threats they faced—deforestation, pollution, resource extraction—were not abstractions.
At Wilderness International, where she served as co-director of science and helped establish the organization's Peruvian branch in 2019, Tolmos brought both precision and passion. She insisted on clean data and sound methods, not out of pedantry but because she understood that donor trust and long-term conservation depend on evidence that holds up under scrutiny. Her colleagues recall her enthusiasm for new tools—canopy cameras, environmental DNA, drones—and her relentless attention to detail in the field. At the Secret Forest Research Station in Tambopata, she would skip the dormitory bed, string a hammock between trees, and spend nights surveying amphibians and reptiles, rising before dawn to help with bird mist-netting. Her husband remembered how she noticed what others overlooked: moss, sea cucumbers, the first light above the canopy.
Tolmos died of breast cancer on June 21st in Barcelona, ten years nearly to the day after her first kiss with her husband under Amazonian stars. She was 37. Her life was brief, but it was lived in service of things that unfold slowly—forest recovery, ecological knowledge, the patient work of protecting places worth keeping.

item.title: Listen to whales to improve connection, care & ocean health (commentary)
item.description:
Two ocean scientists with nearly a century of combined experience are sharing what whales can teach us about communication, care, and the future of marine ecosystems. Marine biologist David Gruber and legendary oceanographer Sylvia Earle reflect on how our understanding of the ocean has evolved—from Jacques Cousteau's 1950s depiction of a silent sea to today's recognition of a world alive with intentional sound, from snapping shrimp to the complex vocalizations of whales. Gruber's work with Project CETI is now attempting to translate whale communication, revealing depths of complexity scientists are only beginning to grasp.
The commentary arrives at a sobering moment. While their decades of work have yielded conservation victories, ocean health is declining faster than protection efforts can counter it. More than 90% of Earth's warming over the past fifty years has been absorbed by the ocean. In a single human lifetime, as our population has grown from 2 billion to over 8 billion, coral reefs, kelp forests, and ocean wildlife have been cut nearly in half. Yet whales offer a counterpoint to this trend: with protection, most whale species are rebounding. These animals, which evolved tens of millions of years ago and share an ancestor with humans from 92 million years back, have thrived without depleting their ecosystem—revealing sophisticated societies with cultural transmission and complex communication.
This story matters because it reframes conservation around a simple principle: understanding fosters connection, and connection fosters care. The authors believe the next decade will determine what future generations inherit from our ocean, and listening—literally—to whales might teach us how to live more sustainably within the systems that sustain us all.

item.title: Nepal’s birdwatchers help monitor wildlife and promote tourism
item.description:
In Nepal, a quiet transformation is taking place among the country's hills and wetlands. Birdwatching is evolving from a niche hobby into a meaningful tool for conservation and community engagement. The movement is led by people like Shankar Tiwari, who once hunted birds as a child near Chitwan National Park but became captivated after seeing a black-hooded oriole through binoculars for the first time. Now a respected guide, Tiwari is part of a growing community helping to monitor the country's more than 900 bird species, including the spiny babbler, Nepal's only endemic bird.
A recent study highlights both the promise and challenges of this trend. While birdwatching is attracting younger enthusiasts and contributing valuable ecological data, only about 37% of birdwatchers share their observations with platforms like eBird or conservation organizations. Many may not fully understand how their records help fill critical gaps in understanding bird populations and habitats, or how the data informs national conservation priorities. Meanwhile, the need for such vigilance is urgent: urbanization, infrastructure expansion, and climate change are altering bird habitats across Nepal, with surveys showing significant declines in species diversity in areas like Kathmandu's Bagmati River corridor.
This story matters because it illustrates how personal passion can quietly fuel scientific discovery and conservation action. It's a reminder that citizen science—armed with little more than binoculars and curiosity—can make a tangible difference, especially in a country where birdwatching tourism remains largely untapped despite its extraordinary biodiversity.

item.title: Why are penguins changing their breeding calendar in Antarctica?
item.description:
Three species of penguins in Antarctica are breeding earlier each year, representing one of the fastest behavioral shifts ever documented in animals responding to climate change. Over a decade of camera monitoring across 37 colonies revealed that Adélie and chinstrap penguins are now starting their breeding season roughly 10 days earlier per decade, while gentoo penguins have shifted their timing by approximately 13 days per decade—the most rapid phenological change ever recorded among birds globally.
Researchers used a network of 77 automated cameras between 2012 and 2022 to track penguin behavior on the Antarctic Peninsula and subantarctic islands. The cameras also recorded local temperature data, showing these colony sites are warming about four times faster than the Antarctic continental average. This regional warming appears directly connected to the altered breeding schedules. However, not all species are responding equally well. Gentoo penguins, with their varied diet and year-round presence near colonies, may be better equipped to handle changing conditions. Meanwhile, Adélie penguins—more dependent on sea ice—and chinstrap penguins—specialized krill feeders—could face greater challenges ahead.
What makes this story quietly remarkable is its uncertainty. While the behavioral shift is clear and dramatic, scientists still don't know whether earlier breeding actually helps or harms these populations, or whether it simply represents the species' attempt to adapt. As breeding schedules converge, competition for space and resources may intensify. The open question—how much further can these ancient seabirds adjust if warming continues—reminds us that nature's responses to change, however swift, may have limits we're only beginning to understand.

item.title: Sightings off Southern Africa suggest blue and fin whales may be rebounding
item.description:
After decades of near-silence, the waters off Southern Africa are witnessing a quiet return. Blue whales and fin whales, virtually eliminated from the region during the industrial whaling era of the 20th century, are being spotted with increasing frequency along the Atlantic coast. A new study tracking sightings in the Benguela ecosystem—a nutrient-rich stretch between Angola, Namibia, and South Africa—offers cautious hope that these ocean giants may be finding their way back to historical feeding and breeding corridors.
The numbers tell a story of both devastation and tentative recovery. Industrial whaling killed an estimated 350,000 Antarctic blue whales and 725,000 fin whales in the Atlantic between 1913 and 1978. The recent study documented only 17 blue whale sightings and 76 fin whale sightings since 1964, but remarkably, 95% of these occurred after 2012. Researchers believe the Benguela's cold, krill-rich waters serve as a vital migratory route, and the uptick in sightings aligns with broader global patterns observed since the end of commercial whaling. Still, Antarctic blue whales remain at just 3% of their original population, while fin whales have climbed to over 30%—far from the 50% threshold scientists consider successful recovery.
This story matters because it captures both the fragility and resilience of ocean life. Whales reproduce slowly, making their recovery measured in generations rather than years. And today's oceans present new hazards—busy shipping lanes, vessel noise, and the threat of ship strikes—that didn't exist a century ago. Yet the fact that these enormous creatures are reappearing at all is a reminder that protection can work, and that the natural world, given time and space, has a remarkable capacity to heal.

item.title: Dusky langurs start using new canopy bridge in Malaysia’s Penang Island
item.description:
In a hopeful sign for urban wildlife conservation, endangered dusky langurs have begun using a newly installed canopy bridge in Penang Island, Malaysia, just two months after its construction. The bridge, whimsically named "The Obscura," was built from repurposed fire hoses and provides a safe passage for langurs and macaques to cross busy roads in Batu Ferringhi, a popular beach resort area. Camera traps captured the first crossing on June 1, 2026, marking a small but significant victory for the Langur Project Penang, a citizen science initiative working to address the deadly consequences of habitat fragmentation caused by rapid urban development.
Malaysia has lost more than half its forest cover since 1900, leaving wild animals increasingly isolated in residential areas and forced to navigate dangerous roads and electric wires. The project's founder, Yap Jo Leen, documented eight vehicle-related langur deaths during just three years of research. The Obscura is the third such bridge LPP has installed, and its relatively quick adoption by the langurs suggests the team is getting better at selecting optimal locations through patient observation and data collection. The bridges mimic natural vines using donated fire hoses, and urban primates appear to adapt readily to these artificial structures.
This story offers a quiet lesson in how thoughtful, community-driven conservation can create practical solutions to the challenges of sharing space with wildlife. The langurs' two-month adoption timeline—faster than the nine months it took at the first bridge—shows that persistence and careful study pay off. With Malaysia's deputy minister visiting the site to consider canopy bridges as a national model, this small-scale project may inspire larger change in how cities accommodate the animals whose homes they've disrupted.
